content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION · 
• 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Paul Kermoyan 
Community Development Director 
70 N. First Street 
Campbell, CA 95008 
June 10, 2019 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-19-107 
Dear Mr. Kermoyan: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating 
your request as one for informal assistance. 2 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Do your financial interests related to your winery located in the same county as the City of 
Campbell, create a conflict of interest such that you are prohibited from processing City land use 
entitlement applications for other wineries to open tasting rooms or bars? 
CONCLUSION 
Due to the large number of existing wineries and tasting rooms in the county where your 
winery is located and the distance of your winery from the City, it does not appear that the decision 
on an application for a winetasting room or wine bar in the City will have a foreseeable material 
effect on your financial interest in your business or your real property. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. I-19-107 
Page No. 2 
You are the Community Development Director for the City of Campbell, and responsible 
for managing the Planning, Building, Housing, Code Enforcement and Economic Development 
Divisions. You have been employed by the City for 10 years and a city planner since 1988. 
You own a winery in the unincorporated area of Santa Clara County, the County in which 
the City of Campbell is located. Your winery is located on your ranch property, and you are 
licensed to make and sell your wine, which you sell from the ranch property. You have a tasting 
room/bar area for this purpose. The City of Campbell is roughly twenty miles from your winery and 
you don't do business in the City of Campbell in order to avoid any perception of conflict. There 
are many wineries in the region and all have individual characteristics which define their identity. 
Your winery is located within a valley with level topography and a vineyard setting which provides 
a sheltered "wine garden" environment. You have horses surrounding the property, lush vegetation, 
swings and games for kids, dog friendly amenities and an overall country ranch experience. You 
believe that this country environment sets your facility apart from a potential wine bar/tasting room 
to be located in the City. You don't believe a wine bar or tasting room in an urban environment 
would compete with the experience you provide and vice versa. According to the web site for 
"Wineries of Santa Clara Valley," thirty-two tasting rooms are located in the county. 
You request informal assistance on your potential involvement in processing of City of 
Campbell land use entitlement permits for wineries that either want to open a tasting room 
or a wine bar, solely in your role as the Community Development Director. No particular 
application is at issue at this time, and you would like to avoid the perception of bias in the 
processing of these applications. 
A winetasting room (selling their wine only) or a wine bar (where all brands of wine are 
sold), requires a Conditional Use Permit approved by the Planning Commission. You state that the 
staff involvement in processing such an application is limited to advising the Planning Commission. 
Staff prepares a report and provides a recommendation. You are heavily involved in forming the 
staff recommendation since you oversee the Planning Division activities and projects. You describe 
the criteria for approval as "somewhat objective." The Planning Commission needs to determine if 
the subject property will be able to support the use, and if the City has "too many" of these types of 
uses in a given geographical area. In certain respects, the staff recommendation is based on 
expertise and land use comparisons. You state that the Planning Commission's record for following 
these recommendations are "at best a 50/50 shot of being accepted." 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. 3 A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
3 We note that your role in preparing the staff recommendation to the Planning Commission may not rise to 
"participating in a decision" as that term is defined in Regulation 187024(b ). Under the Act, participation in making a 
decision occurs when the official "provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting 
the decision without significant intervening substantive review. (Regulation l 8704(b ).) "Significant intervening 
substantive review" has been interpreted to require more than the mere review of the recommendations by superiors, but 


File No. I-19-107 
Page No. 3 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of 
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The financial interests 
implicated by your facts include: 
• 
A business entity interest, where an official has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or 
more in a business entity (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, 
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). 
• 
A real property interest, where an official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or 
more (Section 87103 (b)) including a pro rata share of interests in real property of any 
business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, 
indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater (Section 82033). 
• 
An interest in a source of income, aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in 
value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the 
time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
· 
You have a business interest (including the business as a source of income) and real 
property interest in your winery and ranch. As the owner of the winery and ranch, you also have a 
source of income interest in any clients who make purchases in the amount of $500 or more in the 
_twelve months prior to the decision. 
The standard for foreseeability varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision at issue. A financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable where the 
official's financial interest is explicitly involved as a named party in, or subject of, the decision.4 
(Regulation 18701(a).) For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, the 
financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and is more than a hypothetical or theoretical possibility. 
(Regulation 18701(b).) As your winery is not under the City's jurisdiction, the latter standard will 
apply to the application decisions. 
rather the independent checking of the results without solely relying on the data of the official. (Greenwald Advice 
Letter, No. 1-90-349.) You indicate that the Planning Commission has a history ofrejecting 50 percent of the staff 
recommendations on these types of applications. However, it is unnecessary to make a determination regarding your 
participation in the decisions in light of the conclusion that the decisions will not have a foreseeable material effect on 
your interests. 
4 A financial interest is the subject ofa proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other.entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, or if the 
decision affects a real property interest described in, Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6). 
· 


Business Entity and Source of Income 5 
File No. 1-19-107 
Page No. 4 
The materiality standard for a business interest not explicitly in.valved in a decision is 
whether a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the value of a privately-held business 
entity. (Regulation 18702.1 (b ).) Examples of decisions that may be applicable include those that: 
(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity 
in which the business entity is engaged; 
(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the 
business entity is engaged; 
(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business 
entity supplies; 
(Regulation 18702.l(b)(l) through (3).) 
Real Property Interest 
Regulation l 8702.2(b) provides the applicable materiality standard for an interest 
in real property not explicitly involved in the decision and more than 1,000 feet from the 
property that is the subject of the decision. Under this regulation: 
The financial effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of real property in 
which an official has a financial interest involving property 1,000 feet or more 
from the property line of the official's property is presumed not to be material. 
This presumption may be rebutted with clear and convincing evidence the 
governmental decision would have a substantial effect on the official's property. 
Where your winery and tasting room is located twenty miles from the City and in a county 
known as a winery region, with over thirty existing wineries and tasting rooms, it is a possibility 
that the decision on an application for a wine bar or tasting room to be located in the proximity of 
your winery will have some impact on competition, and the need for your winery's products and 
services. However, given the region's number of existing wineries, the distance involved and the 
differing types of locations, it appears that this impact on the value of either the business or the 
property is more hypothetical or theoretical than a realistic likelihood. Moreover, any possible 
effect would likely be nominal or insignificant giving the facts you have provided. Pursuant to 
Regulation 18702(b ), the financial effect of a governmental decision is not material where the 
financial effect is nominal, inconsequential or insignificant. Therefore, based upon the facts 
5 Where the business entity is the source of income, the materiality standard in Regulation 18702.1 applies. 
(Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).) 


File No. 1-19-107 
Page No. 5 
provided, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the application decisions will have a material effect 
on your economic interests in your winery business, or your real property. 6 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
LKH:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
/ -;(_~J~ 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division 
6 We note that you have not indicated any customers or source of income to your winery and ranch. To the 
extent that a decision or application implicates a customer or source of income to your winery and ranch, you may wish 
to seek further advice prior to taking part in the decision or application.

effect on your financial interest in your business or your real property.2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do your financial interests related to your winery located in the same county as the City of 
Campbell, create a conflict of interest such that you are prohibited from processing City land use 
entitlement applications for other wineries to open tasting rooms or bars?

CONCLUSION: Due to the large number of existing wineries and tasting rooms in the county where your 
winery is located and the distance of your winery from the City, it does not appear that the decision 
on an application for a winetasting room or wine bar in the City will have a foreseeable material 
effect on your financial interest in your business or your real property.2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)
